text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 88398
Submission date: 2025-12-17T00:50:32
Course: COMS W1002 - Computing in Context
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
First half of the class had 3 projects.
Econ section had 3 projects.
I found that the projects were much harder than in-class concepts and exams. 

Lab every week. Useful for review and show the process of how to do the projects.

Midterm was pretty easy. Econ section's final was harder than midterm but not impossible.

Review content:
tbh I would recommend having a bit of coding experience prior to starting the class. The class is split into two halves: in the first half, all the sections are together to learn foundational python skills, and in the second half, you split into your respective sections. I definitely did learn how to code, but since you learn all the foundational skills in essentially 6 weeks, I feel like my skills are still a bit lacking. If you want to actually learn Python, I would maybe recommend an Intro Python course instead of this. 

For econ section: 
The second half of the course was much harder and seemed very targeted for those interested in finance. It mostly covers probability and databases; for our projects, we built monte-carlo simulations, did basic financial modeling, and combed through stock data. Practical and useful concepts, although I found it very advanced for a class that is supposed to be for beginners and non-majors. 

FOR BARNARD ECON MAJORS: this class does not fulfill econ reqs. do not make my mistake. take only if interested.

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 84963
Submission date: 2024-04-11T14:17:21
Course: COMS W1012 - COMPUTING IN CONTEXT REC
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
2 tests: midterm and final. I thought they were very fair tests. I think there was somewhere between 5-7 assignments for this class. There's also a mandatory lab section so make sure to enroll in that too once you enroll in the class. Attendance is recorded for those. I think the weight was ~something like~ 30% assignments, 25% midterm, 30% final, 15% lab attendance. Again that could be totally wrong I don't really remember. Not light, but not heavy work.....just the assignments can take a bit of time to work out and do. Attend the TA OH they're great and always willing to help. Griffin, Rasa and Kevin were my favorites, but they were all great.....just teach a bit differently so might be better to scope out which one's style is most helpful to you.

Review content:
I love Adam Cannon and I loved this class. As someone with ZERO experience in coding or any CS I was scared going into this class. I actually almost dropped this class because I was so scared, but the TA Griffin (shoutout Griffin) talked me off the edge and I'm SO HAPPY he did. This class was not by any means easy. It was definitely tough.....did I walk out of some classes genuinely so confused...yes......did I walk out of some classes feeling like a CS god....yes.......that to me was the temperament of this class. I think Professor Cannon is a great teacher, but to me, more importantly, a great human. He teaches a big class, but if you go see him in OH he is genuinely interested in who you are, your story, etc. which to me is refreshing at Columbia. He is open to answering any questions and creates a very stimulating class environment. My advice for this class is to actually attend class and be present. It can be a tad on the boring side sometimes, but to me the material really started to sink in when I was making sure I understood his every step. Not just sitting in class to sit there. This class is amazing for non-majors looking to dip their toes in the CS pool to see if they like it. To me, the hardest part was the first few weeks.....no one teaches you how to think like a coder which to me is 75% of the struggle. Once you learn to think like one and can figure out how to think to code the solution to a probelm....you're golden. Its NOT AN EASY CLASS (at least for me), but I found it to be incredibly rewarding and I'm glad I took it. Cannon is a gem and so is this class, and I bet the same applies to any classes he teaches.

I honestly recommend this class as a non-STEM person. Some of these reviews scared me off before taking it because they made it seem like if you have no prior CS experience, it'll be impossible. I disagree. I definitely needed to put time and effort into it, but I think that would be the case with any CS class or any class that is entirely new to you. The effort I put in was so rewarding. Coding is honestly really rewarding (as someone who does not intend to ever code again). The class is set up where the first half you learn python with Adam Cannon (who I absolutely loved, such dad humor), and the second half you break off into your context with a different professor. I especially recommend the art context if it is offered. We coded music, animations, and honestly played around. It was hard and I definitely attended more office hours than I have in any of my humanities classes, but really fulfilling for me.

metadata.course_name: Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 84034
Submission date: 2023-05-23T14:50:21
Course: COMS W1004 - Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java
Rating: 5/5

Workload:
Professor Cannon's grade breakdown:
11 programming assignments (lowest gets dropped, counts for 50% in total)
Two Midterm Exams (counts for 10% each)
One Final Exam (counts for 30%)

Some of the programming assignments were challenging to understand and debug, so make sure to go to office hours and ask for help on Ed Discussion. Both Midterms consisted of 30 multiple choice questions. If you are good at Computer Science, work hard in the class, and are not a bad test taker, you will probably pass the Midterms or even do great on them. The averages for Midterms were late 70% to early 80%. The Final Exam had multiple choice questions, short answer questions (they only require you to write a word or a line of code), and coding questions. Professor Cannon made the Final Exam more challenging than the Midterms (I am assuming from my personal experience that most of the challenge was in the coding portion). The class average for the Final Exam was 67.8%, but Professor Cannon curved to a high B. Make sure you study hard for the Final Exam and try your best on it to really benefit from the curve.

Professor Cannon's grading is only somewhat fair. I think the TAs applied the style guide quite harshly when grading the programming assignments, to the point that the grading seemed untransparent to me. There were guidelines students can easily apply from the style guide with minimal or no mistakes; however, the TAs sometimes gave students a grade deduction for something that pertains to style but was not fully explained by them and Professor Cannon. For example, I once received a style guide violation for writing excessive comments, but Professor Cannon and the TAs did not fully explain to me in advance how to write good comments. They did tell us numerous times to write comments in our code.

On average, I spent four to thirty hours per week on assignments for this course. The amount of time I spent on the assignments varied based on their difficulty. I found Homeworks 8 and 9 to be incredibly challenging, so I spent immense time on them (around thirty hours). On the other hand, a few assignments were simple and easier, so I did not have to spend as much time on them. I spent a few hours studying for each Midterm Exam, but I almost always joined the weekly review sessions. To clarify, I mean, apart from the weekly review sessions, I spent a few hours watching other recorded review sessions, asking questions on Ed Discussion and in TAs' office hours, and reviewing my notes and code from class. My scores on Midterms 1 and 2 were 26/30 and 27/30, respectively. On the first Midterm, which was online and all multiple-choice, I accidentally clicked the wrong answer choice, so I would say I really earned 27/30. I studied around fourteen hours for the Final Exam, and this also includes the Final Exam review session a few TAs hosted. My grade on the Final Exam was 81%, which is greater than the class average and two points less than the upper quartile, but the Final Exam was harder than the Midterms.

My final grade in the class was 90.6% or an A!

metadata.course_name: Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java

text:
My final grade in the class was 90.6% or an A!

Review content:
Professor Cannon is a phenomenal professor. He is incredibly approachable, kind, and funny. All of Professor Cannon's lectures were extremely engaging, and he clearly explains concepts (My fellow classmates, remember when he repeatedly emphasizes that instance variables are initialized in the constructor of a class). Despite having prior Computer Science experience, I learned so much about Java in this course. Professor Cannon really tries to help his students learn and complete the assignments. For example, for Assignment #8, which was the Crazy Eights game and the hardest assignment in the course, Professor Cannon made a review video and session to help us with the assignment. He also gave every student in my section a 48-hour extension on that assignment. Professor Cannon is also always open to feedback from students. During the class before the second Midterm, he initially planned to help us with the Deck class of the Crazy Eights game, but after listening to students about their worries regarding the Midterm, he decided to answer students' questions instead. I, as well as my classmates, will admit that Professor Cannon gives challenging assignments. However, Professor Cannon and the TAs, especially Griffin Newbold (he TAs for Cannon in Computing in Content and for his Intro Java course), enjoy helping students and answering their questions both on Ed Discussion and during office hours. I worked very hard on the assignments regardless of their difficulty, and as a result, I learned a lot about Java from Professor Cannon's challenging assignments. The challenge of his course allows students to learn a lot and be amazing programmers. Despite challenging his students, Professor Cannon is never demanding, unkind, or condescending. He cares much more about his students learning and becoming excellent programmers than about their grades. The Final Exam had a 67.8% average, but since the class average grade at the end of the semester was less than the high B cutoff, Professor Cannon curved his students' grades to a high B. Don't worry! Professor Cannon does not want his students to fail, and he never curves down; instead, as I said, he is concerned about his students learning, not about their grades. After each of our Midterms, he even told his students in lecture that if they do not get good grades in tests, they are not bad people :)

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 82157
Submission date: 2020-05-21T17:18:01
Course: COMS W1004 - Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
5 programming projects (all on Codio) 
5 problem sets (usually written problems, sometimes they have a coding question)
The 10 total assignments lead up to being 40% of your grade-- so do them and do them well and you will probably get an A
1 Midterm (25% of the grade)
1 Final exam (35% of the grade)

This is standard from each semester and the makeup of the class did not change when we went online.

Review content:
Honestly this class was not fun at all. I had taken 1006 in the semester before I took 1004 and I LOVED it... my logic was "omg 1006 was so much fun and I learned a lot with great projects and other assignments so I bet 1004 will be just as helpful and I'm going to learn a lot in java!!!" yeah no. At the beginning the problem sets and programming projects were very easy (which I expected and I'm sure people who were taking this as their first CS class appreciated). But like after the first couple of assignments, the projects just got really repetitive and some of them were dumb hard for an introductory level course. Also Codio is the worst IDE. Why they use it, I have no idea.

The worst part is, that I feel like I barely know anything about java. The only thing I took away from this class is that java uses a lot of semi-colons and brackets? I guess I learned a little bit about the internet and Von Neumann architecture? 

The midterm was straightforward I guess, as was the final (but I took this class during the corona semester so idk what the real final would have looked like).

TLDR; if you're not a CS major and just want a fun intro class, take 1006 or 1002. This class is a lot of work with no reward. I spent an entire semester in the class and I still barely know any java. I used guess and check for the projects (like I would write the code that I think would work, check if it complied, look for the errors which were usually pretty minor syntax errors)

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 82110
Submission date: 2020-05-11T19:00:39
Course: COMS W1004 - Introduction to Computer Science in Java
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
Homework (40%) - 6 written problem sets and 5 programming projects. The problem sets took me about an hour each, and I only found projects 3 and 4 to be a little time consuming. Even if you’re new to CS, I think it’s relatively easy to make perfect/near-perfect scores on all of them as long as you follow directions and clarify your doubts on Piazza/during OH as soon as they come up. ADHERE TO THE STYLE GUIDE RELIGIOUSLY! The TAs can be real sticklers about the formatting of your code and the actual files you submit. For example, forgetting to rename a folder before zipping it leads to a point deduction even if you renamed it after zipping.

Midterm (25%) - 10 definitions and 40 multiple choice questions. It’s a cakewalk if you get your hands on past exams. The mean was about a 78 in my semester.

Final (35%) - It mirrors the midterm for the most part, but it also includes a section where you debug code by hand. Pay attention to the code that Cannon goes over in class, as he makes it clear that anything he’s covered is fair game.

Review content:
Full disclosure: I had no exposure to Java prior to taking the course, but I did have decent experience with Python and didn’t have much trouble keeping up with Cannon. The intensity of the workload will be entirely dependent on how much programming experience you already have and/or how fast you can adapt to algorithmic thinking.

Cannon lives up to the hype. He’s an excellent lecturer for an intro class, and he has a great sense of humor. While it’s true that he doesn’t cover the Java syntax as much as some people would like him to, I appreciated his emphasis on conceptual understanding, namely what’s happening behind the scenes of your code. Still, I recommend reading or skimming the corresponding sections of the textbooks before attending lecture to get the whole picture.

Cannon doesn’t exactly hold your hand, but he does provide several resources to help you catch up to speed if you’ve never programmed before. Office hours are held almost all the time thanks to the army of TAs, there are optional review sessions on Fridays, and there’s a separate lab session (again, optional) that runs on Tuesdays and Saturdays (you pick one of the two days to attend regularly). Like most STEM classes, Cannon reserves his A-range for the top third of the class; the cutoff for an A- is around 85% in a typical semester, the cutoff to pass is 55%, and the average is curved to "a high B, not a B+." However, you are nearly guaranteed a good grade if you put in the time and effort, and it’s almost impossible to fail as long as you at least try on the homework.

metadata.course_name: Introduction to Computer Science in Java

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt

Course: Unknown course code - Java
Rating: 5/5

Workload:
I took this class during covid, so they took out one of the psets and projects. 
5 psets, 5 projects, midterm and final.

Review content:
Professor Cannon is, in my opinion, one of the best educators I have experienced in my entire life. He is very very knowledgeable about the subject. But he is also able to transmit that knowledge so that his students understand it. He explains clearly, succinctly, and well. 
Cannon is always available in his frequent office hours, and the TAs (of which there are about 20, and all of whom are amazing as well) also have office hours every day (including Sundays and occasionally Saturdays). 
You get 120 hours of "grace time" in the Spring semester (I believe it's 150 hours in the Fall). You can use all, none, or any amount of this. TAKE ADVANTAGE OF IT. It is an incredible resource to have. 
As for the workload: I had very minimal CS experience coming into this. However, I've still managed to understand everything he talks about because the man is a supremely good lecturer. 
If you have the opportunity to take a class with him, do not under any circumstances switch out! It is worth changing the rest of your schedule to accomodate his class.

Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 81973
Submission date: 2020-04-20T02:42:08
Course: COMS 1404 - Emerging Scholar's Program
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
No homework. Just show up to class. You get 1 free absence. You have to write a short paper if you miss 2 classes, and you’ll fail after the 3rd absence.

Review content:
ESP is not your typical CS class. It’s a small seminar that meets every Friday, and it only counts for a single pass/fail credit. Topics range from applications of basic algorithms to general overviews of machine learning and ethics. I thought the class was a good way to see the specific ways a CS degree can be applied in industry and academia. Cannon is listed as the professor for the course, but he doesn’t actually teach it; everything is handled by the PhD coordinators and the undergrad discussion leaders, but they all have solid CS backgrounds and know their way around the department. Even if you’re set on doing CS, I strongly recommend it as a networking opportunity, as you’ll be able to meet several distinguished faculty members and learn about their research (hint hint).

Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 81385
Submission date: 2019-05-20T22:26:29
Course: COMS W1004 - Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
A programming project due every other week (5 total), and on the weeks the project is not due, a written pm set (6 total). 1 midterm, and 1 cumulative final.

Review content:
I think Cannon was a really good teacher and lecturer. He is entertaining during his lectures, and although he can seem a little unapproachable, he is much less intimidating if you go talk to him during office hours. He also responds to emails quickly if there is a logistics issue. I took AP CS, so the content of the class wasn't that difficult, though there are things to learn even if you did take AP CS, like Turing machines, Dijkstra's algorithm, exception handling, von Neumann architecture, and information about the layers of computer networks. (If you know all of that and took AP CS you could potentially skip to Data Structures, though it was nice to be able to review everything I already knew to really get a firm grip on the material.) I could see the class being a little difficult for people going in with no experience as he doesn't really explain the syntax in depth and you probably will need to do a fair about of self teaching, but conceptually he does a great job explaining everything. I found the midterm and final pretty easy. They were mostly definitions and multiple choice and the final had coding and short answer. Just make sure you pay attention during lecture to all the details. He actually does tell you the answers to some of the harder questions during class. I also really appreciated his organization. Problem sets and projects come out on a timely basis each week. He often codes live for you as well and sends you the code that is done is class, which is helpful.

Review content:
I have truly loved my experience in Intro to Java with Professor Cannon. I had a bit of experience before the class but this class has made me think critically about what it means to be a computer scientist, and how becoming a computer scientist is more than just programming. I think Cannon is engaging and hilarious during lecture, and is great about answering questions throughout class. He is incredibly understanding and looking out for the mental health and well-being of his students. When I have come into situations this semester when I have needed an extension or extra assistance he has been extremely accommodating. He is also accommodating with ODS accommodations. Even if you think you do not want to be a computer science major, I believe you should take this class! Don't let people scare you away from it, it has been my best course this semester.

3 quizzes - harder than they should be for an intro course such as this

Review content:
At the beginning of the semester Cannon will try to trick you into thinking that this class is suitable for people with no programming background. Do not, I repeat, DO NOT listen to him. The first portion of the class is doable, but the second half of the class isn't even covered in the textbook and is next to impossible. His tests are hard. I'm a history major, and I did the humanities track of the course because I thought it might give me some valuable skills. It did not. Instead I was left frustrated again and again and very turned off from computer science. Maybe he is good in Java, but I think he is a poor instructor in Python and should have spent more time and assignments explaining the concepts before throwing us in to very difficult homework sets to "apply our skills."

Rating: 4/5

Workload:
The weeks alternate between a problem set and programming project(Do not leave these to the last minute); 1 chapter of reading a night (DO IT or you will not understand the homework's at all); Midterm in class with definitions and multiple choice (the practice questions he give are very similar to what you will see on the test and if you can get your hands on someone else's midterm from a past semester of his class it is super helpful and similar); Final with definitions, multiple choice, and short answer which includes writing some code; Heads up this is not an easy A course

Review content:
Adam Cannon is a great professor if you're looking into the computer science field. He is a very good lecturer and explains the material very well. Along with that he keeps the class light and interesting so you won't lose focus. This is not a lecture you can skip though (well you can but I would not advise it), the class moves very fast and if you don't go you could miss out on important information for the problem sets and programming projects. This is a great class to take if you don't have any experience in the computer science field. Do not be discouraged if you don't understand stuff in the beginning the learning curve is big for this class. Along with that the TA's are very knowledgable and will help a lot. They all have different office hours as well as on Sundays when you can go to the class room for more help. There also is a Piazza page for the class to ask questions and they will answer fairly fast. Also, the curve is pretty big for this class.

metadata.course_name: Introduction to Computer Science in Java

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 79792
Submission date: 2017-01-26T19:11:03
Course: COMS W1004 - Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
HW: It's weighted quite high, with alternating problem sets and programming assignments week by week. You get some credit if your code compiles and if your style is good, which is good if you can't figure something out once in a while.

Midterm: a mixture of term recall and multiple choice, mostly covering concepts in Java and sorting algorithms.

Final: cumulative, terms, multiple choice, and a few short answer problems. If you do way better on the final he will weight the midterm less for you.

Review content:
Cannon is a very engaging lecturer who explains the material pretty well. His jokes elicit a non-zero amount of laughs consistently, he jokingly thinks the class hates him, and he gives you riddles that have only a tangential relation to the class. 

More importantly, this class provides you with a bunch of background knowledge for CS, moreso than any Java programming proficiency, something that will come in Data Structures. It's a wide breadth with a little depth, but he and the TAs do an admirable job explaining concepts and answering questions, preparing you for the (reasonable) midterm and final.

metadata.course_name: Intro to Computer Science- Programming in Java

text:
Professor: Adam Cannon
Source document: adam_cannon_reviews.txt
Review ID: 79716
Submission date: 2017-01-10T02:11:03
Course: COMS W1004 - Introduction to Computer Science in Java
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
Heavy (8-10 hours per week)
Alternating problem sets and programming projects
Midterm
Final

Review content:
I really enjoyed this class. I had some programming experience in high school, which was very helpful, but there are lots of people who don’t have experience and still do well. If you are interested in the subject and willing to work hard, this class is very doable. Cannon wants you to succeed and so do all the TAs. There are tons of office hours, which you should go to right away if you are confused, especially at the beginning of the semester because computer science builds on itself. My advice is to start the projects early (seriously, look at the prompt before class and then try to do half of it by the next class and then finish before the deadline) and get help often. You might want to look at some online coding courses before hand, but it isn’t necessary. Many people take this class having never looked at code before in their life and are fine, but it is definitely harder because coding is a new way of thinking that might not work for you. Also, it is important to go to class because there were several questions on the final that are based on programs we went over in class but weren’t explained in the textbook.
