text:
Professor: Paul Blaer
Source document: paul_blaer_reviews.txt
Review ID: 23487
Submission date: 2009-12-16T23:57:06
Course: COMS W1005 - Intro to Computer Science- Programming in MATLAB
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
Six homeworks (upwards of 10 hours each, especially from #3 - #5, granted he does warn you) every two weeks or so. One midterm (generously curved, thank goodness) and one final (tomorrow, woo).

Review content:
I had never taken a comp sci course before, and let's just say it was rough. Homeworks took me roughly 10-15 hours to complete (boy did I hate the computer labs in Hartley and Mudd after this course), granted I usually did them the weekend before they were due, but it was painful. 

Professor Blaer moves very quickly in his lectures, flipping back and forth between Editor and Workspace so it was often difficult to write down what he had typed before he switched windows. He would also go back into code and edit what he had originally to create a new example, easy to do on a computer but almost impossible to write down the whole code again with the change (usually very small but really important to his point) by hand before he switched back to the workspace. 
 
I went to every lecture but got lost during histograms and plotting and never really recovered until the last homework. I still don't understand the whole image-histograms-plotting thing, which bodes well for the final tomorrow. An unfortunate fact about matlab; not many people take it unless they are comp sci majors or bme kids (or need to fill the comp sci requirement for seas like me), so help around campus is limited. 

My advice? If you've never taken comp sci before then find yourself a study/homework buddy in either your class or as an upperclassmen who has taken matlab before. Go to lecture. Get used to the idea of spending hours at a time in the computer lab (bring a sweatshirt, it gets cold in mudd). If you get to a point in the homework where you just can't handle it anymore, it's ok to just turn it in (especially if it's past 3am). Ask for help if you've sat in front of the computer for more than an hour without making any progress. Seriously. It's not healthy.

Good luck.

text:
Professor: Paul Blaer
Source document: paul_blaer_reviews.txt
Review ID: 22777
Submission date: 2009-05-14T19:42:17
Course: COMS W1007 - Object-Oriented Programming and Design in Java
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
6 homework assignments, due every 2 weeks, that take 2-4 hours to complete if you have a good grasp of Java (and, I'm told, many more hours if you don't).

1 midterm, 1 final, both fairly predictable and not too difficult.

Review content:
As one of the students that has "practically taken this course before" mentioned below, I should preface this review by noting that if you have any kind of Java experience beyond the AP CS AB level, you should really try to test out of it. I asked about skipping the class and was strongly discouraged by everybody in the department that I talked to; in the end, I think that was a mistake.

That being said, a class that by all rights should have been an unbearable repetition of things I'd already taught myself turned out to be a reasonably good experience. Blaer is a really engaging lecturer and an all-around smart, friendly guy -- he was happy to entertain various questions I had about side projects totally unrelated to the course material, and is really accessible outside of class.

The homework turns out to be enjoyable as well -- there's enough freedom and flexibility in the assignments, particularly the middle few, that you can have fun playing around within the constraints.

All in all, I highly recommend Blaer (and would happily take any other course he teaches), but be wary of signing up for 1007 if you come from a CS background.

text:
Professor: Paul Blaer
Source document: paul_blaer_reviews.txt
Review ID: 22624
Submission date: 2009-04-24T20:44:34
Course: COMS W1007 - Object-Oriented Programming and Design in Java
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
Watch out! The biweekly assignments can easily run to 20+ hours, or even 30 hours.

Review content:
Professor Blaer seems to know anything and everything there is to know about the Java programming language, and practically lives and breathes CS. He's always happy to explain the finer points of Computer Science to us. His office hours, immediately after class, are held at an unusually convenient time for most students and even if it takes him more time than he thought it would, we rarely go away with our questions unanswered. In addition to holding his posted office hours, he vigilantly monitors the course discussion board. His board posts are essential (and appreciated) given the difficulty of his assignments.

The 6 assignments can be quite time-intensive for people who are less than overqualified, including top students from the recommended prerequisite course (COMS W1004). A substantial number of students have a much stronger background than this, and some have practically taken COMS W1007 beforehand. These may be the people who would call the workload "not too bad." The rest of us just get a lot of programming practice. Fortunately, the grading and grading curve are generous. The median grade is usually B+, and this semester it might be A-. 

Lectures are understandable and thorough. Professor Blaer sometimes talks so quickly that note taking becomes a bit difficult, but most of the material is from the textbook anyway. The textbook, _Object-Oriented Design and Patterns_ by Cay Horstmann, explains concepts reasonably clearly but provides inexcusably buggy solutions to exercises. Exams are long and information-intensive, but manageable if you've studied.

Professor Blaer LOVES to make fun of students who procrastinate (he never singles out individual people but talks about Columbia students as a group). He is also merciful toward those who do procrastinate, because of his liberal extension policy. Sometimes he says things about the average student's level of organization (i.e. our lack thereof) that aren't true. I hope his lectures get put on CVN because they are so good, but those parts are cut because they reflect badly on Columbia University and are a lie. Most students are doing well this semester, and our organizational skills are one of the reasons. 

I highly recommend this course, but only if a student has taken COMS W1004 or the equivalent beforehand. Otherwise they might get buried in coursework.

text:
Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 89971
Submission date: 2026-05-25T13:26:58
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
6 Assignments, 1 Midterm, 1 Final. Prof uses Pensieve instead of GradeScope; the only difference between the two is that the former has built in Claude as an AI assistant for participation exercises. 

I spent probably 1.5 hr on each HW, spent 7 hr total revising for each test.

Review content:
Professor Dear is a very approachable, very experienced lecturer who teaches discrete math in a structured way. It is very clear what you need to learn in order to succeed in the course, and I found his programming exercises very interesting and manageable.

Everything in this course (assignments, midterm, final) was extremely reasonable in terms of difficulty and workload. You might need to spend some extra time solving the assignments, but it is worth doing because the difficulty of exams closely mirrors the assignments. Final average was around 70%, but this is typical for a discrete math course because it's the first proof-based course for many. In my opinion, though, the exam was surprisingly light (some questions were trivial), and it felt almost too easy considering the fact he allowed us to bring 4 cheat sheets. If you put in the time understanding the assignment questions and carefully read through the Zybooks, you should be fine. 

TLDR: course content is extremely easy, but exams can require some more careful thinking and problem-solving.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 89834
Submission date: 2026-05-17T05:24:58
Course: COMS W4771 - Machine Learning
Rating: 5/5

Workload:
There were 6 HW assignments and three midterms, all weighted equally. The HW assignments were not super long but were very difficult and would therefore take a while to do. I liked that the last few questions of every HW were applied, (ie. coding in Python with pytorch). There was no coding at all on tests so this just felt like a fun thing to do if you were interested in seeing the concepts your were learning in action and visualized, rather than something extra. The theoretical part of the HWs would be similar to test questions and would be very difficult. The first midterm was mostly pre req material and contained only a few, relatively easy ML concepts so it ended up being far easier than the following two midterms.

Review content:
Machine learning is a notoriously hard class and Tony Dear is a notoriously hard professor, so the combination was about as expected. The material was highly theoretical, though there was more application in HWs than with some other profs. The class was also structured better than other ML profs (in my opinion)

Take the pre requisites for this class seriously. 1) Linear algebra - Most test & theoretical HW questions were basically an exercise in how well you can APPLY linear algebra to prove theoretical ML concepts. 2) Statistics - prob/stats is fine but stat inference is really, really useful, especially for the first third of the class. It is certainly useful to know linear/multiple/logistic regression, ridge/lasso, and MLE before actually taking ML. 3) Multivariable calc - important for convex optimization (Lagrangian, gradients) and neural network back-propagation (partial derivatives/chain rule) 4) Optimization - MATH 2500 and IEOR 3609 are not strictly necessary but are pretty helpful for some parts of the course as well. If you are missing any of (1), (2), or (3), you will likely have a tough time. 

Dear explains the material very very well even if a little quickly. Like with his other classes, you walk out feeling like you learned an immense amount, even if your test grades do not reflect that. As with his other courses, the curve is very forgiving, which I think is a great reason to take this class... focus on learning this stuff and do not worry about your grade too much, it ends up being fine.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 89811
Submission date: 2026-05-16T16:33:52
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
6 homeworks for the entire semester -- each one is long and takes a while, but they're spaced out to at least account for that. If you take the Friday section you will only have 1 midterm (30%) whereas if you take the one with two classes per week, you will have 2 midterms (each 15%). He's very generous with the amount of cheatsheets you can bring to the exam.

Review content:
I liked that he posts his notes online and that the Ed is always very responsive. There's also recitation and office hours, which supplement the content/learning experience. He's definitely a very kind person but I think that the most common criticism -- that his exams are difficult -- is valid, especially as taking the other professor should lead to an easier experience. I believe it's curved to around a B+.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 89723
Submission date: 2026-05-11T20:18:21
Course: COMS W4771 - Machine Learning
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
Three exams in total;
one homework every two week;
Tony set this kind of workload for all of his classes.

Review content:
Take Tony’s class if you want to learn ML in depth. At first, you may not fully understand why you need to learn so many mathematical concepts, because they are difficult and their purpose may not be immediately obvious. But as you go deeper into the field, you will gradually build your own knowledge map and realize how useful those concepts are.
This course mainly focuses on the mathematical foundations behind machine learning. Tony also tries to make the material more applicable by including coding questions that connect the theory to real-world applications.
That said, this course is hard. It is one of the hardest courses I have ever taken, so you should be prepared before taking it.
If you only want a good grade, you may want to take another professor’s version. If you want to learn something about ML but are not sure you can handle the workload, consider taking it PDF.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt

Submission date: 2025-10-01T15:49:24
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
Problem sets took ~6 hours/week. Exams took ~8-10 hours of review.

Review content:
Tony Dear is a very clear and detail-oriented lecturer. He's cares about helping his students succeed, whether that be making extra office hours or a generous curve. However there is a large gap between the examples walked through during class and the homework problems, and a similarly large gap between homework problems and exam problems. Class examples are the foundations but for homework problems you need to learn how to apply your base knowledge. Also reviewing for exams is less about how many problems you do and more about deeply understanding the concepts and solutions for the problems you do. Discrete problems require a level of creativity, so it's more important to just understand what's going on than to crank out extra practice. Problem sets were pretty time-consuming and for harder units like Mod I had to self-study external material to understand. Definitely utilize your resrouces: go to TA and professor office hours. He's hard but fair. I would say take him if you are generally curious, like a challenge, and don't mind putting in the time outside of class to deeply understand the content. If you want an easy A, take Discrete with Ansaf. Final exam was extremely hard, ~110/150 is a good score for an A typically.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 87237
Submission date: 2025-05-22T13:48:49
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
There were a total of seven problem sets, assigned roughly every two weeks. The exams were difficult and often went beyond the level of the homework and recitation questions, this gap became more noticeable as the semester progressed. The median score dropped significantly from the first midterm to the final. However, since the class is graded on a curve, it didn’t impact final grades as harshly as it might have otherwise. 

The main advice for anyone planning to take this class with Professor Tony Dear is simple: show up. Attend every lecture and recitation, start working on the homework early, and make use of office hours. It’s extremely difficult to succeed in this class without putting in the work—unless you already have a strong background in proof-based mathematics. On average, I spent about 12 hours per week on this class. Sometimes it was less, but during exam weeks, it could be as much as 20 hours.

Review content:
Everyone says that the class is really hard—and to be honest, it is quite a bit of work. However, I wouldn’t go as far as to say you should avoid Professor Dear at all costs. Discrete Math is an introduction to proof-based mathematics, so it's no surprise that it feels challenging at first. That said, Professor Dear's class is genuinely informative, which is especially valuable if you plan to do computer science in the future. The course follows a textbook, though a few chapters are skipped. In a nutshell, it was a tough but interesting class with a professor who is eager to explain topics you don’t understand

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 86259
Submission date: 2025-01-02T20:10:15
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
HWs: 10-15 hours each.
Exam studying: a full week of studying is needed
Final is worth 35%. Like bruh.

Review content:
Yeah so this course gave me trauma. 

The content given in lectures were very easy, and I understood it well. But, some of the HW questions were nearly impossible. There were so many times where Dear or the TAs gave out hints, or changed the question, or gave other ways of solving it because it was too damn hard. I clearly remember going to Dear himself to ask a HW question, and even he had to think about it a lot before he could help me. Unfortunately, hard HWs mean even harder exams. The midterms weren't too bad, but the final literally ruined me. I guessed on like half of the questions and wanted to give up. I solidly thought I failed the course.

What's unfortunate is that I love math, and I think I would have really enjoyed this course if the problems were not so difficult. I get that we're trying to be challenged, but at what cost?

If you are conflicted between taking the course with Dear or Ansaf, I'd say you will learn more with Dear, but have a harder time and probably a lower grade than if you take it with Ansaf.

Also, this is not to crap on Dear. He is very nice and kind, but I think he doesn't realize how hard his problems are sometimes. It also doesn't help that the TAs make the HWs sometimes and they underestimate the difficulty.

How to do well (or, at least, not fail):
-Go to the TAs or Dear for HW help. This will save you.
-Fully understand the answers on the practice exams.
-Use textbook problems for the first 2 midterms, but then AI generated problems will help you more for the final.
-Focus on scoring as high as possible on the HWs (meaning, pretend they are the most important things ever)
-Don't give up.
-Know that this is a hard course taught by a hard professor.

My grade breakdown:
HW total: slightly above average
M1: below average
M2: average
Final: below average
Raw score: 75% (this was curved to a B+)

TL;DR: Don't give up, but also be aware that this is a hard course to begin with. Good luck!

Review content:
Professor Dear is a nice guy and clearly knows a lot about his subject, and is very willing to answer questions or help out in office hours. But he also seems (to give him the benefit of the doubt) to lack a certain awareness of students' level of understandings and lives outside of class. For fall break, he made an assignment due on Saturday night, and the midterm was on the first day back to class. For Thanksgiving break, there was an assignment due the Monday after the break. As the content has gotten more difficult, he has not exactly adapted to make sure people are understanding or keeping up, and I don't think I'm the only one who has to do significant review outside of class—plus, extra content is sometimes built into Friday recitations, which are recorded but are not a required or advertised part of the class.

Midterms (2) and homeworks are not terrible, but are certainly generally much more difficult than the examples shown in class. Overall, it's far from impossible, but I've heard much better and easier things about Ansaf's version of the class.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt

6 homeworks (lowest of which is dropped)

Review content:
Going into this class, I'd taken my fair share of Columbia CS Courses. I knew to lower my expectations and expect lots of theory and lots of math. Still, this was an incredibly dull and mind-numbing approach to a subject that is currently upending the way we work, create, and communicate. Dear is honestly un-avoidable in the CS department because he carries so much of it with Discrete, AI, and CLA and I just don't think his teaching style works for me. Lectures are well-organized (positive) but formulaic to the point where it's just so amazingly boring. An uncharismatic, dry CS lecturer isn't a big deal if the homeworks are interesting/make sense. Yeah......no. In this class, homeworks always felt as they were written to punish and trick students, not actually get them to understand the material or find it any more interesting. I spent more time trying to figure out what the questions were even asking than solving the questions themselves. Exams were demoralizing, and felt harder than the practice exams. At least with CLA he curved generously though so we'll see.
I know you need to walk before you can run, and that's probably the reason behind keeping everything so abstract and theoretical. But one little taste of how AI can be actually cool to work with/build projects with would be nice. Maybe that's the big brain point of the class? If you're not super into the math and getting tricked on homeworks then AI isn't for you?

This class is SO theory-heavy, you will spend most of your time staring at extremely long statistical equations with little explanation on the slides, rendering them almost pointless unless you are able to decipher them on your own.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt

Submission date: 2023-01-15T06:58:36
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: 1/5

Workload:

Review content:
While Professor Dear was a nice person, this class was one of my worst experiences at Columbia so far. I felt the lectures were boring and barely helpful. Our homework assignments were ridiculously difficult and graded very strictly. It was insanely hard to make logical leaps from what was learned in class and apply it to the homework and I spent more time trying to understand what some problems were even asking then solving them. Multiple homework assignments had incorrect information or confusing and roundabout prompts to trick or confuse you maybe but just lead to questions that didn't make any sense. The first exam was reasonably difficult but the average was around an 80. Professor Dear saw this as his opportunity to increase the difficulty even further with the average on the second exam being around a 50. The excuse for the problems being insanely difficult was because it was open note but there were a couple problems that used formulas that didn't even exist in the notes and weren't covered in lecture. The final exam had a similar result. While the class was heavily curved in the end, I feel I didn't deserve the A- I somehow ended up getting because I feel I still don't understand half the concepts we covered in class because of the brutal difficulty and terrible experience. This class has turned me off of all future theoretical CS classes at Columbia. I would not recommend Professor Dear for any class as I heard this is a common experience. From looking at previous course content from Professor Ansaf, that seems to be the way to go for Discrete Math.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 83327
Submission date: 2021-05-01T00:00:00
Course: COMS W4701 - Artificial Intelligence
Rating: None/5

Workload:
No workload text provided.

Review content:
I took this course with Prof Dear but this review is more about the course than the professor.
COMS 4701 is very much "plug-and-chug" and leaves very little room for self-discovery. If this does not match your learning style, you will probably feel like you're wasting your time. Because there is so much hand-holding involved, it's easy to score well without doing all that much work. That said, I think this course is a great introductory course for non-majors or people just starting CS.
Material--very broad overview of AI and high-level discussions of concepts. To get a sense of what the book is like, you can just check out the Wikipedia page on "Artificial Intelligence" or any of the topics on the syllabus. You will not find much more depth in 4701.
Quizzes--the quizzes are mostly multiple choice and it's all open book. Extra study time generally is not rewarded as there's not that much depth involved. Also, expect some wrong multiple choice answers to be adversarial in nature so be prepared to second guess your response all the time.
Homework--About 75-80% of the code is written all ready, and the remaining code is not difficult to implement. Most of it was translating pseudocode into python. The short answer responses were also not terribly interesting. "Is this implementation more efficient than the other", "Is this guaranteed to find a solution", etc.
Grading--There is a curve, but it's probably not necessary since everyone is scoring in the 80s and 90s. The grading rubric is well defined and expectations for each assignment/quiz are clear. 
I would recommend this course to people without a background in CS or math. The professor is very knowledgeable and the TA's are helpful. Or, if you want an easy A, go for it.

Professor: Tony Dear
Source document: tony_dear_reviews.txt
Review ID: 82200
Submission date: 2020-06-09T07:38:18
Course: COMS W3251 - Computational Linear Algebra
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
A lot.

Review content:
On the surface, this class is great. Professor Dear is organized, nice, and offers a lot of office hours for his students. This will especially be evident during the first week, where the material is trivial.

However, I do not recommend taking this class for several reasons. The first is the fact that the lectures just spit out different theorems in a convoluted way, where he is just regurgitating statements from the textbook. So you think you can just learn the material from the textbook or online because this is probably not your first time experiencing terrible lectures at Columbia. In fact, it might seem tenfold better, where you can skip class and do the problem sets and learning yourself. So you try the textbook, and realize it's just as convoluted as the lectures, because well, he was regurgitating those statements. And so you go online to learn the material yourself, which works fine for actually learning linear, until you receive the problem sets and realize it's just as convoluted. I can't emphasize enough that the problem is not the fact that you can linear on your own, but to do well in this class, you have to answer those convoluted questions that you won't find any source teaching you how to do. And so you got the TAs, but they themselves admit that they do not know how to solve the problems. Most people have large groups of friends to try to crack the problems together. If you do not know linear, you will be screwed, because well, you will actually be trying to learn linear and find practice problem sets on your own, before trying his problems. Again the problems are convoluted because of his style, not because of linear itself that will be added as a twist. The workload is a lot, one problem set a week and a lab every two weeks. You will really need a light workload to try his class without any knowledge of linear. The most frustrating part is the fact that you will think "This is great. I love to be challenged!" but then realize you will be slaving away only to solve his specific problems because Professor Dear likes to overcomplicate simple concepts. You will spend so much time but learn nothing new. Only take this course if you know linear and want to be challenged to solve those sets, and perhaps not learn anything new. Save yourself and take the regular linear if you can before the curriculum change. If you cannot because you have to follow the new curriculum, learn linear before taking this class and make sure you know a lot if people in this class to do well.

The professor is not good at explaining the reasoning behind the mathematics involved in the course and after a certain point, it's hard to follow his lectures when all you hear are math terminology with little to no connection to how they're used for AI. He tries to be as helpful as possible, but when you're that confused - I don't think it'll help.

Homeworks are annoying given that there are revisions to the questions 10 days into the assignment - and are fairly difficult when compared to the examples solved in class. They're also weighted weird, with some 5 point questions taking 2 minutes to solve, while some 5 point questions take upwards of 1-2 hours with a ton of calculation involved. The programming sections are nice though, so no complaints there.

He's a super nice guy and tries to make everyone understand the subject, but he's not a good professor for this particular course. Especially when compared to the source material from Berkeley

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi
Source document: ansaf_salleb_aouissi_reviews.txt
Review ID: 86239
Submission date: 2024-12-31T14:27:25
Course: COMS W4701 - Artificial Intelligence
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
The class had 5 coding assignments and 5 conceptual assignments (50% total), and two exams throughout the semester (25%) that effectively served as “midterms” without any final when I took it. To my knowledge, this setup doesn’t apply anymore and the second exam had gotten pushed to later on in the semester and became a final. The assignments were staggered such that the conceptual assignment was always due before the accompanying coding assignment. The lowest coding and conceptual grades also got dropped at the time. The conceptual assignments involve calculations, short-answer, etc. related to the topics we went through for the course, while the coding assignments involved applying these concepts for Python code. The assignments we did were:
* comparing search algorithms (breadth-first search, depth-first search, etc.) for number sliding puzzles
* backtracking with optimization for solving sudoku puzzles 
* minimax for deciding a move for 2048
* using a neural network for sign language sign classification
* a 5th assignment that I unfortunately don’t have the file for at the moment as I skipped it (my apologies)

These assignments get reused between semesters, and I would say the 2048 algorithm was the most difficult, as it involved testing out different values as part of functions that you need to implement. The sudoku and number-sliding puzzles were the second-hardest, then the neural network one was the easiest out of the four I completed for the class. The exams, however, were pretty rough. I was able to do decently on the first with moderate studying for it, but I admittedly did much worse on the second since I couldn’t finish. However, the course gets curved accordingly, and these exams take up relatively low weight compared to other classes. To that end, the work is a lot more doable to be able to get an A compared to Prof. Dear, as well as other CS classes in the department – do what you will with that information…

Review content:
AI with Prof. Ansaf was solid. For context, I took it during the Fall 2023 semester. It served as a nice opportunity to learn concepts and strategies that serve as a basis for artificial intelligence and gradually transition to working with actual AI technology. The balance between coding and conceptual assignments also served as a way of putting these concepts we learned in lecture into practice (though they got a bit tedious at times). Though I’ve never attended her office hours, Prof. Ansaf seemed approachable. She also was fairly engaging as a lecturer.

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi
Source document: ansaf_salleb_aouissi_reviews.txt
Review ID: 83236
Submission date: 2021-01-01T00:00:00
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: None/5

Workload:
No workload text provided.

Review content:
This was a great class. Ansaf is an excellent professor and cares a lot about her students. I took the fall 2020 version of the course which was entirely online. Ansaf did a terrific job adapting the course to an online format. 
I am talented at math (A+ in the course) but still got a lot out of the course. I think prior reviewers do future students a disservice by saying the class is too easy. 
So here are the exam stats for my semester: midterm average was 88.6 and the final exam average was 78.4. Clearly many students struggled with the material on the final exam. It was a very fair exam -- my point is that the class is not trivial if you really want to master it. 
Some students have said that it is easy to hurt your score by making a careless mistake. The class is not akin to a heavy computational Ordinary Differential Equations course where a single mistake is easy to make and will throw you off. If you are making mistakes on the problems in this class, you generally do not have a perfect understanding of the concepts. And these are key concepts in mathematics. 
If you actually pay attention in lecture, read the notes, and also read the text (the Scheinerman text is solid), you will learn a few important concepts even if you have a solid math background. And these are very key concepts in math. The coding assignments are good Python practice. 
Even if you place out of the course, I would recommend taking it. It's a very crucial course in one's intellectual development and it's enjoyable.

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi
Source document: ansaf_salleb_aouissi_reviews.txt
Review ID: 83234
Submission date: 2021-01-01T00:00:00
Course: COMS W3203 - Discrete Mathematics
Rating: None/5

Workload:
No workload text provided.

Review content:
Ansaf is one of the kindest professors you’ll meet at Columbia, but she’s a mediocre teacher; she’s too prone to glossing over the meat of the material. Everything you need to know can be easily found in the lecture notes she posts at the end of each week, so attending lectures is pointless. I think attendance trickled down to 20-30 students after the midterm.
I will say that the course felt rushed near the end. Discrete was recently bumped up from 3 credits to 4 credits because the syllabus was revised to add more targeted prep for classes like AI. However, it’s clear that Ansaf was not ready for this change. We went through the first three chapters at a snail’s pace before the midterm, and then Ansaf had to cancel a full week’s worth of lectures because she got COVID. As a result, the second half of the semester felt strapped for time, and we blitzed through the rest of the material. It got to the point where I don’t feel adequately prepared for classes that require Discrete as a prerequisite even though I got an A in this class.
On the TAs: it seemed like most of them were TAing for the first time. Piazza posts took forever to get answered, and it usually took a few weeks for graded work to be returned. I never attended their Friday recitations, but the problems they made were generally harder than the homework or exams. Being able to solve these problems means that you truly understand the material, so be sure to do them if you know you’ll need Discrete in the future.
At the end of the day, I’m not sure if I can recommend Ansaf’s version of Discrete. It’s one of the easiest classes at Columbia and doesn’t require a ton of work, but don’t expect it to sufficiently prepare you for higher-level math or theoretical CS classes. If you have the option of testing out, I think doing so and taking a more advanced course in its place would be a better use of your time unless you desperately need the GPA boost.

Review content:
I took this class Spring 2018

Professor: I think that professor Ansaf is great. Really, despite all the comments written below I still think that it is unfair to moke professor only because she is a naturally kind person. She was always approachable and her course was extremely well organized.

Organization:
- first of all, you can see all the topics she is going to cover on courseworks from the very first day of the class
- you can also see the dates of upcoming exams/midterms to organize your schedule (you may think that it is always the case but once you get more experience with Columbia, you may realize that it is NOT)
- there were always availability of lecture notes handwritten and typed (also very helpful and rare at Columbia)
- there are two books for this class which cover all the material but students usually do not notice the existence of the second one: it is only for "inference topic" but you do not have to buy it. All of them are always available at the library

OHs:
I personally never went to OH and I cannot say anything about TAs for this class, but the overall organization was so great that I even didn't have to! Everything was in books and lecture notes.

Exams:
Midterms were great: you can have a cheat sheet with formulas you may forget. Then you do not need to study formulas: you basically have to UNDERSTAND the material. Moreover, it consists of numerous small problems: it never happens that the problem is worth 40-50-60% of the exam (as it happens in other classes: but you realize it again only with more experience at Columbia).
That is why when you cannot solve something - you still will be fine.

Overall feeling:
It was the only course where you didn't need to stress out and be scared as hell before exams if you missed a lecture because everything was organized so well that another student even didn't need to go to a lecture. Probably, that is why everybody decided not to attend lectures and then to complain.

Overall feeling:
It was the only course where you didn't need to stress out and be scared as hell before exams if you missed a lecture because everything was organized so well that another student even didn't need to go to a lecture. Probably, that is why everybody decided not to attend lectures and then to complain.

Teaching style:
This class is big: one section is around 150 students. And it is always hard to explain material if there are so many people. 
If you go to the lecture and keep attention to what the professor explains - you will definitely enjoy her teaching style.
If you stay in the last row and spent half of the lecture on facebook - you probably will not enjoy her teaching style.
That is why to enjoy this class I would suggest to go to the lecture and try to listen to the professor, to attend her OHs: 
it is the only way you will really enjoy the Discrete. Believe me, Ansaf is great: you just have to keep attention to what she is saying. 
In any case, if compared to other classes, you always will be well equipped with all the materials needed.

Course: COMS W4701 - Artificial Intelligence
Rating: 4/5

Review content:
I learned a lot in this course. 

I didn't really know much about the theoretical underpinnings of AI and in the semester I took the course (Spring 2017), we went through a bunch of unsupervised/supervised learning techniques. The lecture slides were very good and I found her lectures also quite understandable. 

I thought the exams were quite fair as well, with the Midterm being a bit long but still doable. The Final wasn't cumulative so the amount of material to cover in both tests is actually reasonable. If you go through the lecture slides, you should be fine.

The assignments were a different animal. I really underestimated how much time the 1st assignment would take, which was a lesson I learned the hard way (I basically spent Friday-Sunday writing code all day). The 2nd assignment was frustrating because your entire grade was based on 10 test cases (and I thought the times I ran tests on my own computer, I scored better). Thankfully, the last three assignments are definitely easier and took me way less time.

Overall, I'd say it was a pretty good introduction to AI. The curve seemed to be fairly forgiving based on the grade I got.

Review content:
I took this Fall 2017. 

Lecture: I know that most students stopped going to lecture at the end of the semester because they found it useless, but I disagree. Although she does post power point slides summarizing what she teaches in lecture, they are a fair amount of helpful examples that she goes over in class which are not included in the power points. These in-class examples are also closely aligned with what she puts on the exams. The overall consensus within the department is that Ansaf is a nice person, but a so-so teacher. This is true. Her lectures were average in every respect: organization, clearness, etc. I was a student that always asked questions in class and she was always willing to stop what she was doing, unlike other professors, to answer my question and make sure I understood. 

Homework: Took me about 5-7 hours for every homework assignment. If I got stuck, I would go to the TA office hours for help—at OH, the TAs (at least the ones I went to) were incredibly helpful and often just gave me the answer. Homework is doable if you pace yourself, ask for help from your friends/TAs at OH/TAs on Piazza. 

Exams: The exams are EASIER than the homework, and as I said before the questions on the exams are closely aligned with the ones she goes over in lecture (again, go to lecture). 

Overall, I would recommend this class. Ansaf's kindness/approachability, combined with a generous curve, make this class worth taking despite the fact that her teaching is so-so. You will learn.

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi
Source document: ansaf_salleb_aouissi_reviews.txt
Review ID: 79992
Submission date: 2017-05-07T18:02:36
Course: COMS W4701 - Artificial Intelligence
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
1 Midterm - 20%
1 Final - 30%
5 Homeworks - 50%

Review content:
Before I write this review, I would like to preface it: Professor Ansaf is probably the nicest, most committed teacher I have ever had at Columbia. She was incredibly personable, and always had time during her office hours to help or chat. 

So...I didn't think this was a good class. I know a lot of seniors took this class because it was supposed to be relatively easy where you still learned a lot. Unfortunately, neither of those were true this semester. This could be because this is the first time in two years that this course has been offered, or perhaps because she (very nicely) expanded the class to 275 students, so there was less one-on-one time with instructors. As it was, the Professor would show up late (despite her best efforts) every class because of a meeting directly prior to class. That's unfortunate, because it means the first 5-15min of class were used for setting up instead of lecturing. I think if the Professor knew she would be teaching a class at 1:10pm, she should have scheduled her meeting for another time (or vice versa). 

Additionally, the class was incredibly disorganized. The homework instructions were generally incredibly vague and because of that on Piazza there would be -- I kid you not -- 50-80 unanswered piazza posts during homework times. The homeworks were also badly managed, as they were more rushed in deadlines towards the end of the semester. Also, there was a time where a homework was due the same time as the midterm, which was incredibly distracting and in my opinion badly managed. Considering how much time the homeworks took, it was relatively disorganized schedule-wise.

The exams weren't much better - instead of testing the actual content of the class slides/lectures, they tested your speed in problem solving. If you're able to do each problem in about a minute to 5 minutes you'll be fine -- but that was not typical. If you take the exams, I recommend that you first focus on the highest weighted problems first -- aim for the max points in the least time, not about going in depth. The practice problems are good for general formatting, but not on timing. If the professor had added more practice problems for exams I think that would have helped. 

Finally, the TAs were generally useless. Of the about 10 TA's, all of whose office hours I went to, only about 3 were consistently able to answer my questions and help me on my homeworks. Jimmy/Ruicong was the best TA so I hope he stays on if this class gets taught again. 

In general, I wouldn't take this class again. I didn't feel like I learned anything, and the homeworks were just implementing algorithms in a really convoluted way.

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi

Course: COMS W4701 - Artificial Intelligence
Rating: 2/5

Workload:
5 Homeworks - 50%
1 Midterm - 20%

Review content:
As a senior, this is possibly one of the worst classes I have ever taken in the Columbia CS department. Full disclosure: I got an A in this class, so this isn't just whining.

Professor Ansaf is super nice but incredibly disorganized. She never showed up to class on time, and every class had "technical issues" that would last the first 15 minutes of lecture time. Furthermore, when she would teach, it would be rushed and very high level, when what you needed to know on the exams and homework were much more detailed. Again because of her disorganization, the first half of the class was very laid back - there was very little work and only two homeworks. Then, for some godforsaken reason she decided to assign homework 3 (the 2048 game, in my opinion, the hardest homework because of the necessary optimization problems) and have the midterm two days apart. It was endless sleepless nights because the homework took so goddam long and so did studying for the midterm. Her practice exam was only slightly helpful, and neither were her lectures for the midterm.

Side note: her exams don't test knowledge. They test speed. The midterm exam was absolutely ridiculous because it had about twice as many questions as were possible to answer in that time period. It was super frustrating, especially since none of the students knew that coming in, so a lot of people absolutely bombed that exam when we actually knew the content. When I went to talk to her about it, she said she wasn't going to help because everyone was graded on a curve. Okay, thanks for that nonexistent help. 

Finally, the last half of the class was incredibly rushed, because she realized she hadn't taught any of the topics she needed for the homeworks. So instead of giving us time for the assignments by spacing them out, she instead gave us an assignment every week. The homeworks are incredibly time-consuming (because you always have to optimize over and over again) so you barely had any time at the end of the semester to do anything. Finally, the final again was ridiculous. 

All I can say is that I hated this class. Professor Ansaf was disorganized and there were only 1 or 2 TAs that were actually helpful. In essence, take this class only if you're prepared to learn everything on your own, and get no support from the professor besides a curve.

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi
Source document: ansaf_salleb_aouissi_reviews.txt
Review ID: 78321
Submission date: 2015-01-16T16:21:34
Course: COMS W4701 - Artificial Intelligence
Rating: 3/5

Workload:
Weekly lecture 2.5 hours + 4 Python programming assignments (45%) + Class participation (5%) + Midterm/Final Exam (50%) + lot of opportunities to score extra credit.

Review content:
Prof Ansaf is very friendly and the class was interactive. There was a good balance of traditional AI topics such as agents, search algorithms, CSPs and logic with a focus on machine learning in the second half of the semester. The prof mainly used her slides but there were some chalk board sessions as well. The slides were very well prepared and Prof Ansaf takes you through the math in a very simplified manner. The slides are sufficient and the text book can be used for any reference reading.

There were four assignments and all of them had Python programming. Basic programming knowledge is sufficient for this class as Python is easy to pick up. There was a Python recitation taken by one of the TAs in the beginning of the semester and it was very helpful to get started. The assignments were interesting (application of AI to games like 2048, mazes etc) but time consuming - as you can keep trying to improve the performance of your program. Definitely the best way to understand the intricacies of the algorithm though and the prof was kind enough to give additional time on the assignments when required. There were a lot of opportunities to score extra credit if you needed to catch up on a bad performance. We had one quiz and a lot of suggested reading material if you were interested. The course covered a lot of topics - AI and ML related. But the midterm and final were both open notes so you could focus on application rather than memorizing formulae or algorithms. The exams definitely made you think but as long as you understood the content it, the problems were solvable. The final grading formula was very generous and took the best performances into account for most students.

The TAs were helpful during office hours and on Piazza. Especially close to exam time, the response time of the prof on Piazza was great which helped a lot.

The professor balanced heavy course material with lighter "historical moments of the day" and current events in AI which made the two and a half hour class easier to get through. We also had a guest lecturer in the last class. All in all the professor really cared and took great initiative to give us more information than strictly the course content and made the classes more fun.

Professor: Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi

Workload:
4 homework+1quizz+1midterm+1final
The overall aggregation is as follows:

Review content:
I took this AI class with Ansaf. I like her teaching style and her class. She focus on the searching algorithm and machine learning for most time which are quite interesting to me. It is not easy to cover all the machine learning algorithms in less than half semester so that she taught a little bit fast in the machine learning part. The after class studying will be necessary if you want to grasp these algorithms. I also like her homework. She have assigned four homework, all programming in python. Most of them are very practical problem(for example to program a AI for 2048 game) and some of them are very challenging. 

It is hard to say the importance of this class to me. Honestly I expect more in this class because firstly I have taken machine learning before and I want to have a systemic understanding about how to build up an AI. But after taken this class, what I have learn and what I can understanding is not clear enough. I cannot say that I have a clearly and systemic understanding about how to build up a practical AI. Maybe it is because the artificial intelligence is a quite wild field that cannot be cover by only one class in one semester. If I can arrange the topics in this class, I will set this class as a higher level class and the machine learning class should be required before this class. Then the professor may discuss more in AI rather than take most of time to discuss machine learning and algorithm. 

All in all, Ansaf is a quite instructive teacher. For those students who haven't taken machine learning before, this class is very useful and you can learn a lot in this class.

(written by Z.H.)

Professor: Jason Nieh
Source document: jason_nieh_reviews.txt
Review ID: 88549
Submission date: 2025-12-21T15:55:35
Course: COMS W4118 - Operating Systems I
Rating: 1/5

Workload:
50% HWs lowest dropped (mean like a 40, good luck even getting a 100), 20% midterm (mean 40), 30% final

Review content:
Jason seems like a genuinely kind and well intentioned instructor, but the lecture content did not adequately prepare students for the exams. The exams were far too difficult given the level of instruction provided. They required a much deeper and more precise understanding than what was covered in class. He briefly went over portions of the Linux source code, yet the exams expected detailed, low level analysis far beyond those discussions. The exams were also extremely open ended, making it difficult to know whether you were even on the right track while answering. Given how challenging the homeworks already were, this made the exams feel unnecessarily punishing rather than a fair assessment of learning.

I expected this course to be challenging, but the homework assignments were far more punishing than anticipated. The average for nearly every homework being around a 40 is very concerning and should have been a clear signal that something was wrong with the structure of the course. Despite this, the exams were still made extremely difficult, which is hard to justify when the majority of students were already struggling to succeed on the assignments.

A major issue with the homeworks is that none of the individual parts provided any test cases, so there was no way to check whether you had implemented each part correctly before moving on. If you unknowingly failed an early part, you were essentially screwed for the remainder of the assignment. Since the homeworks often consisted of 12 interconnected parts worth 100 points total, a single mistake early on could cause everything else to fail and result in a zero.

There was also no reliable way to test your code in the same way it was graded. If your submission failed even the first hidden test case, you received a zero regardless of how much correct work you completed. Hours or days of work could be completely dismissed because of a single issue you had no way to detect. The homework specifications themselves were extremely unclear, with significant ambiguity between what was written in the spec and what was clarified later on Ed. Overall, the assignments and rubrics felt misaligned with the level of guidance provided. At the very least, partial credit should be awarded given the time and effort required. As it stands, the homework experience was the most frustrating I have had in a CS course at Columbia.

Professor: Jason Nieh
Source document: jason_nieh_reviews.txt
Review ID: 87866
Submission date: 2025-11-23T13:53:46
Course: COMS W4118 - Operating Systems I
Rating: 4/5

Workload:
6 (50%) Homeworks, can drop 1. 1 (20%) midterm, 1 final (30%) (cumulative). Homework average tend to vary hw 1 is easiest and hw 3/4 is hard - average floats around 40/100. Exam average ~45. Median curved to B+

Review content:
The hardest CS class in the department (if not in the country). Professor Nieh, is very warm and kind teacher. Knows the material inside out and has been teaching for the past 30 years. If you are taking OS, take it with him. He uses board and teaches the concept really good. He also goes over Linux code and tries to explain concept through code. What makes OS hard is: 1. it is a dense topic meant for 2 semesters so you are rushing through everything 2. Homework are intentionally made hard with verbose but unclear guidance for you to figure things out. They do it intentionally, so you go to office hours. Unless you understand the material, coding is a nightmare. You need to read ~1,000 lines of code every homework to code 10 lines (expect to write ~500 lines in C for some hws) - unless you want your code to deadlock. From my experience, I think they use a grading script, meaning that if your code don't compile you get 0, and their test cases are very hard (test, test, test your code). It is a team based assignment and choose your teams wisely. Unless this is your only class, have good team else you're going to suffer a lot. If you do not vibe with your teammates, switch on the next homework (they let you do that for every homework). start early, in fact initial kernel compilation takes ~2 hours, so last minute work is impossible. Lecture is recorded with a policy that ~60 % attendance required to access the lecture recordings. No notes. However, Ed discussion is your best friend. Professor Nieh answers all questions on Ed. Start early, unless you understand your material nothing helps. You can use AI to explain concepts and cite it on your reference (but even then they do not help as much in fact it misleads you). the key is to learn it yourself. Unfortunately, reading books won't help as much than to attend lecture. His exams are fair, not tricky like AP, in fact if you attend lectures thoroughly and know what he is doing you can get 100 %, however each question holds more points than you think - they give partial credit unlike AP except for the hw (no parial credit).

Professor: Jason Nieh
Source document: jason_nieh_reviews.txt
Review ID: 86406
Submission date: 2025-01-11T20:05:30
Course: COMS W4118 - Operating Systems I
Rating: 5/5

Workload:
Total points out of 1000. Approx. 550 is B+. 650 was an A.

6 Homeworks drop 1, 500 points. 
Midterm 200, Final 300.

Exams are kind of like AP, where you can lose a lot of points even if your gap in understanding is not significant, so your fundamentals need to be clear. Huge curve, so don't lose confidence off of the raw scores. As long as you're staying with the median of the class, you should be okay.

Lock in, you'll learn a lot.

Review content:
I have never learned more with such a level of hands-on experience before in my CS education. Most other operating systems classes are almost entirely theory focused and their assignments barely scrape the surface of the essence of OS. In Professor Nieh's class, the six homework assignments work to build a story for your OS understanding. This class is hard, but the value in learning is extremely high.

There is really no way for anyone to gauge the true difficulty of the class based on reviews. Yes, most of the reviews here say that this is an extremely challenging class, but that materially doesn't really mean much until you are deep into the class. This class is challenging, but with proper warning and preparation, I don't think it is impossible at all. There is SO much help provided for this class, more than I have ever seen for a class. Office Hours everyday of the week (except Saturday) with extremely well-versed TAs, and an Ed Discussion Board where TA's respond within a few hours max. If there are reviewers saying that this class is impossible or that they needed to self-study material, they probably did not utilize office hours.

Don't take this class if you aren't interested in systems programming.

Don't take this class if you aren't interested in systems programming.

Warnings and Advice: 
1. Take this class seriously. It's three credits, but has a really high workload. I took this in a semester where I only took four classes total and it was still a stretch for me. 
2. You need a good team. You can switch your team every homework, so don't hesitate to do so. This is not the time for being nice. Focus on your learning. I personally switched my team twice and it ended up being okay. They say the limit is three people to a team, but the assignments are doable with two people that have good communication and drive. 
3. Put the ego aside, go to office hours. So many people refuse to go to office hours and waste an unbelievable amount of time trying to get something to work, only to get low grades on the homeworks (me early on). The TA's are extremely helpful, and so are your classmates! OH is a great place to work on homeworks too, since its a pretty collaborative environment. Again, reviewers that say they had to self-teach themselves, probably did not go to office hours.
4. The homeworks are not one-shot-able. You will need the two weeks. You can "get things working" in a few all-nighters if you procrastinate, but you definitely won't get the grade you want in that case. 
5. Exams are pretty difficult to actually study for. The best thing you can do is review homework solutions and try to think about what each part of each homework aimed to fundamentally teach you. 
6. You need to have really clear C fundamentals. You will read significantly more C code than you will write, but in both cases you should be able to read, understand, and write C well. 
7. Lectures are extremely important. It can be difficult to follow tracing kernel code in class, so definitely rewatch lecture.

Professor: Jason Nieh

The assignments require tons of prep going through kernel source code, and you can either get stuck with a group where everyone essentially works on their own on the same exact thing or does nothing -- both cases undesirable. You can change groups after each assignment if you'd like, but odds are you're going to get stuck with more undesirables. I learned a lot with the assignments and invested probably 50-80 hrs into each of them, but it was not a good experience working with others in the class who were not on the same page, and the grades were absolutely not reflective of the effort put in.

Review content:
The homework assignments are a massive time investment, especially if you are stuck with a group that shirks its responsibilities. Even if your code runs and appears to do what is required, the autograder has edge cases which will decimate your grade and essentially invalidate all your effort. The lectures are mostly the professor clicking through the kernel source code on bootlin to the point where you don't even know what's going on and wonder why you're even still showing up. He switched things up about halfway through the semester and seemed to focus more on illustrating concepts on the blackboard, which was a lot more useful. The exams--not sure what the point of them is. The median scores are like 30/100, so apparently if you don't answer exactly as they expect you to, you'll be getting a failing grade, even if you did okay on the homework assignments and understood the concepts. I essentially had to teach myself the concepts using the textbooks and still managed to fail the exams even though I was pretty confident in my understanding of each subject. The course grade is curved, but talk about discouraging. Is the goal to teach or to weed people out here?

Review content:
“Long is the way and hard, that out of Hell leads up to light.”
― John Milton, Paradise Lost

Operating Systems I is without a doubt the hardest course I have taken in my life, and will likely be the hardest you've ever taken as well. That being said it is also the greatest course I've ever taken and I would 100% do it again if given the chance.

The course teaches you (surprise!) how a modern operating system works, using Linux as a case study. You'll read and modify the Linux source code so you walk away not only with an understanding of how an operating system works but how to modify the Linux kernel for your own projects. Now, that sounds cool (and to be honest it is) but this leads us to the fact that the inherent difficultly of the course lies not in the subject matter, but in the sheer volume of material you need to learn in 4 months. If you are considering taking this course, you should be a) be VERY comfortable coding in C (if you did well in AP and understood the material you're good to go) and b) taking a light course load. On the second point, I'm a SEAS student who took 4 classes the semester I took OS, and they were all relatively easy. I still ended up having no free time as I was always either working on the homeworks for OS or the homeworks for my other classes.

This brings us to the homeworks (Oh God). There are 6 total, and typically you get 2 weeks per assignment (I say typically because towards the end of the semester he gave our class some extensions on the deadlines). Starting with homework 2 you're put into groups of 3. Be sure to choose a good group, but more importantly, if you get a bad partner, SWITCH TO A DIFFERENT ONE NEXT ASSIGNMENT. Trust me, you don't want to go through this course alone, look out for yourself. Be prepared for a lot of stress, confusion, and late nights. Once you get to the middle of homework 3 it's not an exaggeration to say you'll be working 30 hours a week on these. Homeworks 1(writing a minishell) and 2 (something with task structs) are easy, homework 3 (process state changes) is the hardest in terms of difficulty (plan to drop this homework), homework 4 (writing a scheduling class) and 5 (something with page tables and memory) are easier than homework 3 conceptually but still have heavy workloads, and homework 6 (file systems) is easy (note this homework will be easy only in comparison to the previous homeworks, this would still be pretty difficult in a normal class).

This brings us to the homeworks (Oh God). There are 6 total, and typically you get 2 weeks per assignment (I say typically because towards the end of the semester he gave our class some extensions on the deadlines). Starting with homework 2 you're put into groups of 3. Be sure to choose a good group, but more importantly, if you get a bad partner, SWITCH TO A DIFFERENT ONE NEXT ASSIGNMENT. Trust me, you don't want to go through this course alone, look out for yourself. Be prepared for a lot of stress, confusion, and late nights. Once you get to the middle of homework 3 it's not an exaggeration to say you'll be working 30 hours a week on these. Homeworks 1(writing a minishell) and 2 (something with task structs) are easy, homework 3 (process state changes) is the hardest in terms of difficulty (plan to drop this homework), homework 4 (writing a scheduling class) and 5 (something with page tables and memory) are easier than homework 3 conceptually but still have heavy workloads, and homework 6 (file systems) is easy (note this homework will be easy only in comparison to the previous homeworks, this would still be pretty difficult in a normal class).

Now to the lectures. One of the only reasons I think people tolerate the insanity of the course's difficulty is the fact that Jason Nieh is an amazing professor. He's a great lecturer, and by the end of the semester you'll have internalized all of his catchphrases for explaining linux source code (drinking game idea, take a shot every time he says "under the covers"). In the inevitable event you don't understand something from the homework, go to his office hours. Not only will you get help on your assignment, you'll get to discuss the homeworks with other people in the class, because everybody shows up to his office hours.

If you're debating taking OS, the most important thing you need to know is if you love systems programming. And I really mean love, because only love can prompt a perfectly sane individual to dedicate all of their free time and happiness to writing a custom page table that's >1000 LOC only to get a 17/100 because you forgot to build for ARM architecture (unfortunately true story). That's not to say you can't have doubts, but if in the first two weeks you're only moderately interested, drop the class. Like the quote above indicates, OS is the Hell. That's not just my experience, it's like that for everybody. But it's 1000% worth it in the end. You'll make friendships based of shared trauma that will last a lifetime (the friendships and the trauma), you'll get a great understanding of how personal computers actually work, and you'll walk away an incredibly strong programmer. OS is long and hard, but if you stick with it it'll lead you to Heaven.

Professor: Jason Nieh
Source document: jason_nieh_reviews.txt
Review ID: 82983
Submission date: 2023-01-06T04:17:57
Course: COMS W4118 - Operating Systems I
Rating: 5/5

Workload:
6 homeworks, 1 gets dropped. At the beginning of the course, Professor Nieh said that Homework 4 (scheduler) is known as the most difficult. However, this year, he changed Homework 3 and oh my god, those 2 weeks spent on Homework 3 were the most arduous 2 weeks of my life at Columbia. Nothing worked. My computer froze unpredictably and intermittently. Google didn't help. Stack Overflow didn't help. It just didn't work no matter what we tried. But after that brief 2 weeks in hell, my understanding of the material improved drastically, so much so that Homework 4 did not feel difficult at all and ultimately, our Homework 3 was dropped. Aside from Homework 3, all the other homeworks were challenging but pretty interesting. I definitely felt frustrated while doing them, but never bored. In addition, the homeworks taught me how to use git version control, how to understand a large codebase, and how to collaborate with others, all of which I believe will be helpful later on. 

Also, the exams are very, very, very tough because you have to write code on them. I am pretty sure our final exam had a ~30 average. But the curve is generous and if you make sure you understand the homework solution code, you can't do too poorly.

Review content:
This course is definitely the most difficult one I've taken at Columbia, and many of my classmates agree with me. Regardless, it was a great experience, and towards the end, it wasn't even that bad! Professor Nieh is a very clear lecturer that is incredibly knowledgeable about the material and established in the field. He is also very friendly- GO TO HIS OFFICE HOURS!! The material itself is not too difficult to grasp, and the two textbooks, OSTEP and LKD are very helpful. I also thought the material was pretty interesting- I felt like I finally understood what my computer is doing on a foundational level. I also particularly enjoyed Professor Nieh's lectures where he goes through examples of synchronization on the blackboard, as those made the difficult concept quite clear. As many of the previous reviews pointed out, Professor Nieh likes to go through Linux source code in class. Those lectures can be quite boring and confusing if you haven't looked at the source code beforehand or started the homework, but once you're caught up, they're usually very relevant to the homework.

Course: COMS W4118 - Operating Systems I
Rating: 2/5

Workload:
Staff will give a license key for virtual machine software, where then you can code everything in your VM instance.
There are six HWs, one midterm and one final. The lowest HW score will be dropped. How they looked like and how should you deal with them are discussed in the content section.
To compute your grade, add up five highest score of your HWs, your midterm score times two, and your final times three. If you achieve a score of 700+, well A/A+ here you go. The average is usually 550-ish, which is a B+.

Review content:
Well, I've always heard other students saying that OS is the hardest CoSci course in Columbia. So, I'm like, well, since I've taken most of my required courses, so why not give OS I a shot? And oh boy, all these rumors about how hard this course is are real...
This class is the HARDEST 4000 level course I've ever taken, it's like CLT plus advanced Algo plus complexity theory plus Computer Networks(by Prof Ethan, of course). So yeah, that kind of hard. AP, analysis of Algo I, and lots of other CoSci courses which are labelled as challenging basically becomes...trivial in front of this one. So, please consider carefully before you enrolled this one, or else it will drop a GPA bomb on you.
So yeah, do not take it unless:
1. Your track requires it, well then it's no escape here lmao
2. You really likes OS
3. You are a C-language god and knows how to do kernel programming. Well under this case, be my guest, take it:)
Oh btw, even if you have to take OS, if you are a master student, you should take it at the last semester, where you only need to take two courses. This course has a workload of 2.5 courses, so yeah, don't take it when you're enrolling 4!

Don't get me wrong, Prof.Nieh is very good at teaching. He knows how OS(specifically, Linux here) works, and he delivered course contents crystal clear. If you found something vague and unclear, you can easily get your answer by ganking the Prof after class, post an Edstem post or just read the textbook. So conceptually, it was not that bad.
Also, the grading process of this course is also very fair. There will be auto testers plus manual review of your submission with clear rubirc. So it's unlikely you got point deducted without a proper reason.

Also, the grading process of this course is also very fair. There will be auto testers plus manual review of your submission with clear rubirc. So it's unlikely you got point deducted without a proper reason.

At this point, you might ask, oh so what make this course so hard? Well, its workload and complexity of HWs.
The first one is about writing a simple shell. You just need to make sure it perform just as what the statement defines. Watch for memory leaks! Don't Malloc without freeing things. Valgrind should be very useful here. I mean, you've taken AP before and you better know all of this without me telling you here, or you're just not ready for OS.
You should aim for 95+ when doing HW1. Trust me, if you didn't do well or find it too hard for you, you probablty should drop this course. Having a good grade in HW1 will 'pull up' you a little bit in your final grade calculation.
The second one should be implementing a system call. It will take a while to compile for the first time. So I suggest you to compile the given skeleton code as soon as it was released. Same rule applies for all following assignments. HW2 is hard, but not hard at all if you compare it with following HWs. Make sure you understand all these Linux and C language conventions, such as Linux List head and all these Macros. They will be very useful:)
Also starting from HW2, you will work with a team of 3. You need a WORKING TEAM, which can be either a team without slack teammates or a team with at least one god-tier carry who seems to spoke C language in his/her/their daily life. If you didn't get a good team for HW2, either you can rush it out with your own wisdom(it's still possible for HW2), or you gonna scew up, which is kinda of fine at this point. However, for ANYONE who wants to do well, or at least decent, my rules are:
1. Get a working team!
2. Get a working team!
3. Get a working team!
You want to cut steak with a plastic spoon? NO YOU DON'T! I cannot stress more on this. They'd lock me up in an asylum, yet I'd still say, "GET A WORKING TEAM!" So yeah, if your HW2 screwed up cause your team does not work, DUMP THEM IMMEDIATELY. Prof.Nieh approves this, you should write an email to him politely, and he will arrannge you a new team. If that does not work. Repeat the dumping process. Don't afraid to be an A-hole in this class, trust me, a non-working team will make you feel much worse then just dumping them. Don't trust your teammates "Oh I will do better next time", THEY WON'T and YOU SHOULD NOT TAKE THE RISK. Thus, unless you yourself is a god-tier teammate, eject immediately when you feel something is off.

Ok, back to HWs. HW3 is likely to be a HW on state of processes, preemptions and threadings. Start early on this one! Or else you might get your first 30/100! Well, at least you can try to figure things out by browsing through linux source codes. "Source codes are ground truth!", said by Prof.Nieh. Also, try to arrange your teammates s.t. for any OH during the week, there will be at least one guy in your team be there and querying the TA. TAs are extremely helpful(or else how could we survive through this course?) and you SHOULD ASK THEM QUESTIONS. Your codes does not compile? Ask TAs! You got a Null-pointer-Exception? Ask TAs! You got some unexpected Core-Dump? Ask TAs! Trust me, AT should be a solid rule you follow, just like RTFM. And it's gonna helped you a lot.

You got some unexpected Core-Dump? Ask TAs! Trust me, AT should be a solid rule you follow, just like RTFM. And it's gonna helped you a lot.

Alright, assume you survived through HW3 with a working team, HW4 is about scheduling, and believe it or not, it is not that hard(comparing to 2~6). Linux scheduling classes are pretty much...OOP, so I think it is really not that bad. The only crappy thing is, you might not be able to boot if you mis-write some codes. Hence, make sure you use snapshots of your VM software. Trust me, you don't want to re-install your instance, re-compile for one hour just because you wrote some code that broke your previous VM instance. Otherwise, you should actually aim for 80+ for this one.

Ok, here comes HW5. It's likely to be one about memory paging. Remember I said you should get familiar with Macros, well here you gonna use them a lot in order to walk the page table. Again, start this one early. Prof.Nieh might allow you to submit this HW after the Thanksgiving(If it's fall term). But during Thanksgiving, there will be NO OH held by TA/Prof. Thus, finish it early, or good luck trying to figure why this/that page fault/seg fault occurs by yourself.

HW6 is about Filesystems. It's a long but not that hard one. However, at that point, you will have tons of upcoming final and due on final projects. Hence, a working team is really important here! Tell your teammates not to bother using crappy excuses such as 'oh I have a final so I cannot do anything'. EVERYONE IS BUSY DURING FINAL WEEKS AND THIS IS NOT AN EXCUSE. Don't feel defeated when you open files provided by TAs and found them to be completely empty.(Very cool man, actually giving out a skeleton code without any bones.) Try to make progress everyday and you are likely to survive through this final HW.

Alright, so much with HWs. Now, time to talk about exams. THEY ARE HARD. If you found sample exams to be easy, IT'S A TRAP. Make sure you re-read assigned chapters s.t. you understand conceptual questions. More importantly, review previous HWs! It's likely there will be coding questions which asks you to modify some previous HWs. If you screwed up some of previous HWs, make sure you read solutions! I would say that solution codes given by TAs are hard to understand, as they might made changes to Linux Kernel in multiple files across different folders. So, make sure you spotted all of them and understand why. TAs are likely to use some Linux Kernel functions that are not covered during lectures, you will want to gank them during OH and post Edstem posts s.t. you can understand what happened. Understanding HWs will help you at least average in exams, thus please take my word on this.

Merry Christmas:)

Workload:
(50% ) 6 homework assignments. There's a group component, which focuses on programming for the Linux kernel, each of which takes easily more than 20 hours of your time to finish (and often more). There's also an individual written component, which actually takes a maximum of 2 hours to finish.

Review content:
Given the last review for Professor Nieh was written in 2013, I figured it is worth to share some more thoughts on this class.

This class is everything they say it is. Nieh will teach you everything you need to know about Operating Systems, and then some more. His lectures are fast-paced, dense and sometimes difficult to keep track of. However, he is a very good lecturer with an impressive command over the material. He will answer several questions during class, which sometimes gets frustrating as he digresses. If you pay attention, you will learn a lot, but you will often have to back up the concepts with the textbook to actually master the material.

And then there are the homeworks. Oh God. Nieh will talk about the concepts in class and he will talk about the homeworks a little bit, but to be honest, you are all alone. They involve writing code into the Linux kernel. Not only do you have to read A LOT (I mean, A LOT) of C code to figure out what you need to do, you also have to be really good at C to actually implement what's expected of you in a reasonable amount of time. Expected to spend 10 hours figuring out what you actually need to do before you write a single line of code. Expect to actually skip homework on other classes to finish these assignments. They will make you a great coder (especially if you want to be a systems programmer), and you will actually master reading Linux source code by the end, but it's a ridiculously rough patch. You will also have to demo your completed homework to a TA after it is done.
